result.chapter_summary: Appendix II provides a methodological overview of the 2008 French study (Note d’Information 08-38) which tracked a twenty-year decline in student performance between 1987 and 2007. Hirsch uses this data to argue that reading is more sensitive to school-based knowledge curricula than math because reading relies heavily on vocabulary and general knowledge acquired in the home, making school-provided knowledge the primary lever for equity.

claims.text: The target population for the 1987 French study consisted of fifth-grade pupils enrolled in the public sector.
claims.source_passage: The target population in 1987 was that of fifth-grade pupils enrolled in France in the public sector.

claims.text: The percentage of primary school students in the French private sector remained stable at approximately 14 percent between 1987 and 2007.
claims.source_passage: It should be noted that the percentage of primary school students in the private sector remained stable over the twenty years (about 14 percent).

claims.text: In 2007, French students in the private sector achieved results equivalent to those in the public sector across literacy, numeracy, spelling, and grammar.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, in 2007, students in the private sector achieve results equivalent to those of the public sector, whether in literacy, numeracy, or in spelling and grammar.

claims.text: Centralizing and processing student responses via a web server allows for more reliable corrections and quality measurement than teacher-led scoring.
claims.source_passage: This system allowed for more reliable corrections to measure quality.

claims.text: The overall decline in mathematics performance in France between 1987 and 2007 was more than twice as large as the decline in reading.
claims.source_passage: The overall decline in math was more than twice the decline in reading: –.84 standard deviation in math, compared to –.37 standard deviation in reading.

claims.text: The decline in educational equity, measured by the spread between demographic groups, was four times greater in reading than in math in France.
claims.source_passage: The increase in spread between the top and bottom groups in math was .1 standard deviation, while the increase in spread in reading was four times as great: .4 standard deviation.

claims.text: Poor schooling has a significantly greater negative impact on equity in reading than it does on equity in math.
claims.source_passage: Poor schooling has a greater negative effect on equity in reading than in math. This finding is important for schooling as an agency of equal opportunity.

claims.text: Mathematics is primarily a school subject where instructional effectiveness tends to decline for all students uniformly.
claims.source_passage: Math is primarily a school subject. If the effectiveness of math instruction declines for some, it tends to decline for all.

claims.text: Reading performance is dependent upon general knowledge and vocabulary size, making it both a school and a home subject.
claims.source_passage: Reading is both a school and a home subject. It depends upon general knowledge and vocabulary size.

claims.text: Direct word study and skills-based curricula cannot achieve a significant increase in a student's vocabulary size.
claims.source_passage: But a significant increase in vocabulary size cannot be achieved through direct word study and a skills curriculum.

claims.text: A knowledge-based curriculum is far more effective at promoting equity than a skills-based curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The knowledge curriculum can contribute far more to equity than the skills curriculum can. France has now demonstrated this forcefully.

claims.text: The adoption of whole-language methods led younger French teachers to reject the more effective phonics method for teaching decoding.
claims.source_passage: Indoctrinated in the superiority of whole-language methods, younger teachers spurned the far more effective method of phonics in teaching decoding skill.

claims.text: The rejection of systematic grammar and spelling exercises in favor of 'natural' approaches contributed to the decline in French student writing skills.
claims.source_passage: They also spurned exercises in grammar and spelling in favor of more “natural” approaches.

claims.text: The percentage of ten-year-old French students making more than twenty-five writing errors doubled between 1987 and 2007.
claims.source_passage: An analysis of the writing errors from ten-year-olds in the two periods showed that the percentage of students who made more than twenty-five errors doubled in the period, from 6 percent to 12 percent!

claims.text: The 1987 school selection, though limited to five jurisdictions, was statistically representative of the parent population.
claims.source_passage: In 1987, the selection of schools took place in just five jurisdictions, but the sample was found to be representative of the parent population.

claims.text: Decimal multiplication and division were removed from the French primary curriculum between 1987 and 2007.
claims.source_passage: multiplication and divisions of decimals were excluded as they are not in the present curriculum.

claims.text: Valid longitudinal comparison of student performance requires the identical repetition of test instructions across different years.
claims.source_passage: Instructions for the test were repeated identically in each year.

claims.text: To maintain assessment validity and prevent student fatigue, no more than one test sequence should be completed in a half-day session.
claims.source_passage: It was required not to complete more than one sequence in a half day.

claims.text: The variability in inter-rater agreement for reading items (dropping as low as 80% for some questions) has a negligible impact on final performance metrics.
claims.source_passage: The rate was more variable in reading: it dropped to 80 percent for some items, mostly due to hesitation between total and partial success, but had little impact on the final measure of performance.

claims.text: Schools must maximize their contribution to student knowledge and vocabulary in order to fulfill the social aim of equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: The school contribution to knowledge and vocabulary is thus key for the aim of equal opportunity, and needs to be maximized.

claims.text: Between 1987 and 2007, France experienced a simultaneous decline in the effectiveness of instruction for both knowledge acquisition and decoding skills.
claims.source_passage: A confounding factor in this reckoning is that the effectiveness of instruction in both knowledge and in decoding declined in France.

claims.text: The increase in the performance spread between demographic groups was quantified as .1 standard deviation for math and .4 standard deviation for reading.
claims.source_passage: The increase in spread between the top and bottom groups in math was .1 standard deviation, while the increase in spread in reading was four times as great: .4 standard deviation.

evidence.description: Note d’Information 08-38 from the French DEPP, comparing performance of CM2 (5th grade) students in 1987, 1997, and 2007.
Comparison of standard deviation declines showing math declined -0.84 SD while reading declined -0.37 SD.
Data showing the spread between demographic groups increased by 0.1 SD in math but 0.4 SD in reading.
Analysis of writing errors in ten-year-olds showing an increase from 6% to 12% in the high-error group (>25 errors).
Reliability testing during the 2007 French survey where one out of ten images was corrected by two markers, showing inter-rater agreement of 95% (math) to 80% (reading).

concepts.author_definition: The statistical distance (measured in standard deviations) between the performance of the top-performing demographic groups and the bottom-performing demographic groups.
A subject, like mathematics, whose content is primarily acquired within the formal schooling environment, making its outcomes more uniformly dependent on school quality.

A subject, like reading, that relies heavily on knowledge and vocabulary acquired in the domestic environment, making it more sensitive to socio-economic disparities unless the school compensates with a knowledge-rich curriculum.
Pedagogical methods (often associated with whole-language) that prioritize an 'unfolding' of skills over direct instruction in phonics, grammar, or spelling.
A sampling plan where schools are selected at random and all students within those schools participate in the evaluation.
The total group of students (e.g., all fifth-graders in the public sector) from which a representative sample is drawn.
The ability to translate written text into spoken language, specifically associated with phonics instruction in this context.
An educational approach focusing on the acquisition of general knowledge and vocabulary as a means to achieve academic equity.

cases.name: France 1987–2007
cases.description: A twenty-year natural experiment in which the French education system shifted toward American-style pedagogical methods (whole language, naturalism). The result was a significant decline in overall student performance and a sharp increase in the achievement gap, particularly in reading.
key_passages: The target population in 1987 was that of fifth-grade pupils enrolled in France in the public sector.
France has now demonstrated this forcefully.
Indoctrinated in the superiority of whole-language methods, younger teachers spurned the far more effective method of phonics in teaching decoding skill.

actors.name: DEPP (Direction de l'évaluation, de la prospective et de la performance)
actors.context: The French agency responsible for the evaluation of education policy and the production of the 2008 report.

actors.name: Younger French Teachers
actors.context: The cohort of teachers Hirsch claims was 'indoctrinated' in whole-language methods and consequently abandoned phonics and grammar.
